Name: pac_bayes_low_latency_inference
Title: PAC-Bayes Framework for Spectral Stability Bounds in Low-Latency Machine Learning Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds, we can develop a novel approach to low-latency machine learning inference that prioritizes both accuracy and efficiency. Our approach will focus on deriving enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference, while minimizing hidden-state perturbation and enforcing strict wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ have explored adaptive mixed-precision training and quantization methods for efficient model deployment. However, these approaches often rely on heuristic-based methods or require significant computational overhead. Our proposal differs from these works by leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds to derive a principled approach to low-latency inference, with a focus on rigorous empirical validation and mechanistic transparency.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel approach to low-latency machine learning inference, leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols. Our approach prioritizes both accuracy and efficiency, minimizing hidden-state perturbation and enforcing strict wall-clock budgets. We will develop a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework that directly targets ML inference theory, and evaluate its performance on a range of benchmark datasets. Our goal is to deliver a principled, efficient, and scalable approach to low-latency inference, with applications in real-time decision-making and edge computing.
Experiments: Evaluate the performance of our PAC-Bayes framework on benchmark datasets such as ImageNet and CIFAR-10
Compare our approach to state-of-the-art low-latency inference methods, including SNIP and AMP-ViT
Investigate the impact of spectral stability bounds on the robustness and efficiency of our approach
Develop a range of evaluation metrics, including latency, accuracy, and throughput, to assess the performance of our approach
Risk Factors and Limitations: The development of a principled approach to low-latency inference may require significant theoretical and empirical efforts
The complexity of our approach may limit its scalability to very large models or datasets
The reliance on spectral stability bounds may introduce additional computational overhead, potentially impacting latency
